title: WARDEN Domain Scenario Library
description: Offline scenario templates used to expand domain questions into likely actors, signals, source hints, and competing hypotheses.

templates.title: Taiwan Strait blockade or quarantine pressure
templates.summary: Cross-strait coercion scenario where military exercises, navigation restrictions, inspections, and market signals point to a blockade or quarantine track rather than routine signaling alone.

hypotheses.title: Blockade preparation is increasing
hypotheses.statement: Observed activity is consistent with preparations for a coercive maritime or air-access blockade around Taiwan.

hypotheses.title: Activity remains coercive exercise signaling
hypotheses.statement: Observed activity is best explained by crisis signaling or exercises that stop short of sustained access denial.

hypotheses.title: Semiconductor supply effects are the primary transmission path
hypotheses.statement: The most decision-relevant effect is semiconductor and maritime-logistics disruption even if military escalation remains limited.

analystPrompts: Separate declared exercise zones from observed vessel rerouting.
Check whether semiconductor guidance changes are attributed to demand, logistics, or political risk.
Require at least one official source and one independent maritime or market source before escalating.

templates.title: Korea semiconductor and battery supply-chain stress
templates.summary: Korea-focused industrial scenario where export controls, critical-mineral drawdowns, supplier substitution, and corporate guidance indicate stress across semiconductor and battery inputs.

hypotheses.title: Upstream material shortage is binding
hypotheses.statement: Stress is driven primarily by critical-mineral or specialty-material availability rather than downstream demand.

hypotheses.title: Control-regime change is binding
hypotheses.statement: Export-control or licensing changes are the key driver of Korean supply-chain disruption.

hypotheses.title: Buffers and substitution are absorbing the shock
hypotheses.statement: Supplier substitution, stockpiles, or demand softness are preventing a material disruption.

analystPrompts: Separate semiconductor, battery, and critical-mineral evidence instead of treating supply chain as one bucket.
Check stockpile language against customs volumes and company guidance.
Look for third-country routing and supplier-qualification evidence.

templates.title: Sanctions and export-control cascade
templates.summary: Economic-security scenario where new designations, license rules, and trade-flow shifts create cascading procurement and compliance risk.

hypotheses.title: Rule changes directly constrain supply
hypotheses.statement: New or clarified controls directly restrict goods, tools, software, or end users needed by affected firms.

hypotheses.title: Evasion and rerouting are rising
hypotheses.statement: Trade-flow and corporate-pattern changes indicate attempts to route around sanctions or controls.

hypotheses.title: Compliance noise exceeds material disruption
hypotheses.statement: Observed changes reflect compliance adjustment and reporting noise rather than binding supply constraints.

analystPrompts: Anchor claims to exact rule text, designation date, controlled items, and named end users.
Compare policy signals against customs and corporate guidance.
Avoid inferring evasion from one trade-flow anomaly without corroboration.

templates.title: Maritime chokepoint disruption
templates.summary: Logistics scenario where shipping-lane friction, insurance premiums, port notices, or vessel rerouting threatens energy, semiconductor, and industrial supply chains.

hypotheses.title: Physical chokepoint constraint is binding
hypotheses.statement: Routing, port, or canal constraints are materially reducing throughput or increasing lead times.

hypotheses.title: Market risk pricing is the leading signal
hypotheses.statement: Insurance and freight pricing are reacting before confirmed physical disruption appears.

hypotheses.title: Delay is localized and absorbable
hypotheses.statement: Observed disruption is local, temporary, and unlikely to transmit into broader supply-chain stress.

analystPrompts: Compare AIS route changes against port and navigation notices.
Separate insurance premium movement from confirmed vessel delays.
Trace which sectors depend on the affected lane and whether inventory buffers exist.
